Two general quidelines to apply in determining whether it would be 
permissible to use this method of reporting are: 
(1) receipt of 
the in-kind services are on-going during the late reporting period 
and would result in repititious reporting; (2) the candidate or 
committee has the ability to estimate the value for the entire late 
period with a reasonable degree of accuracy. 
To illustrate a situation in which this method of reporting would 
be acceptable, if a candidate or committee is receiving use of a 
phone bank, administrative services, or other types of in-kind 
contributions on an on going basis, the candidate or committee will 
be capable of making a close estimate of the value of the services 
which will be received during the late reporting period. 
By filing 
a late contribution report prior to or no later than 48 hours after 
receipt of the first $1,000 of in-kind services, and reporting the 
total amount to be received during the late period, the intent of 
the law will be fully served by providing information to the voters 
aoout the source of contributions received just prior to the 
election. 
When the late contribution is reported on the next regular campaign 
statement, if the actual amount received differs from the estimated 
amount reported on the late contribuion report, that fact should be 
noted on the campaign statement. 
In addition, and most importantly, if the value of the in-kind 
contribution received durlng the late period substantially differs 
from the estimated amount reported, another late contribution 
report must be filed within 48 hours of learning of the 
discrepancy, amending the original late report to disclose the 
correct amount. 
I hope this has adequately answered your question. 
Please contact 
me if you have any fUrther questions. 
Sincerely,

Re: 
Reporting Nonmonetary "Late" Contributions 
Dear Jeannie: 
Pursuant to our telephone conversation of December 1, 
1982, I am asking that you confirm in writing that it is 
permissible for filers who anticipate nonmonetary contri-
butions of $1,000 or more during a late filing period to 
estimate the value of the nonmonetary contributions once for 
the entire late filing period and file one late contribution 
notice. 
The one notice would cover the period for late 
contribution notification. 
Please direct a copy of your response to me to the 
Political Reform Division of the Secretary of State's office 
so that they may be apprised of the Commission's advice in 
this matter. 
Thank you for your prompt attent'~-r0 this matter. 
ft 
\JI 
\ 
KAP:tc 


State of Califomia 
Memorandum 
TO: 
Bob Steele 
DATE: January 28, 1983 
Political Reform Division 
FROM: 
Jeanne Pritchard 
Technical Assitance and Analysis Division 
RE: 
Late Contributions 
This is in reply to your memo dated December 30, 1982, and our 
subsequent conversation, regarding the method of reporting 
non-monetary contributions received during the "late contribution 
period," as set out in my letter to Kirk Pessner dated December 17, 
1982. 
In that letter we provided a procedure for candidates and 
committees to report the value of in-kind services which are 
provided on an on-going basis during the late reporting period to 
file one late contribution report prior to or within 48 hours of 
receipt of the first $1,000 in services, indicating the estimated 
amount which will be received during the entire late period. 
This method was devised to eliminate duplicative reporting when 
in-kind services will be received on an on-going basis; the filer 
know that the value will exceed $1,000; and the value of the 
in-kind services which will be provided during the entire late 
period can be determined with a reasonable degree of accuracy. 
The way this method will typically be used will be that prior to or 
at the beginning of the late contribution period, the candidate or 
committee will file a late contribution report covering the entire 
late reporting period, and indicate the value of the in-kind 
services to be provided during the entire period. 
However, we 
advised that the late report may be filed prior to or no later than 
48 hours after receiving the first $1,000 of in-kind services. 
Therefore, in order for filing officers to apply the 48-hour 
requirement, we will indicate to filers using this method that they 
must also indicate the date on which the first $1,000 in in-kind 
services has been or will be received. 
Thereafter, no additional late contribution reports will be 
required unless the amount received substantially exceeds, or is 
substantially lower than, the estimated amount reported. 
If either 
of these circumstances occur, another late contribution report must 
be filed within 48 hours, amending the prior report to indicate the 
correct amount of the in-kind services. 
Please contact me if you have any further questions. 
I

sections.question: Pursuant to our telephone conversation of December 1, 1982, I am asking that you confirm in writing that it is permissible for filers who anticipate nonmonetary contributions of $1,000 or more during a late filing period to estimate the value of the nonmonetary contributions once for the entire late filing period and file one late contribution notice.
sections.conclusion: Two general quidelines to apply in determining whether it would be permissible to use this method of reporting are: (1) receipt of the in-kind services are on-going during the late reporting period and would result in repititious reporting; (2) the candidate or committee has the ability to estimate the value for the entire late period with a reasonable degree of accuracy. By filing a late contribution report prior to or no later than 48 hours after receipt of the first $1,000 of in-kind services, and reporting the total amount to be received during the late period, the intent of the law will be fully served by providing information to the voters about the source of contributions received just prior to the election. If the value of the in-kind contribution received during the late period substantially differs from the estimated amount reported, another late contribution report must be filed within 48 hours of learning of the discrepancy, amending the original late report to disclose the correct amount.
sections.question_synthetic: May candidates and committees report nonmonetary in-kind contributions during a late filing period by estimating the total value for the entire period and filing a single late contribution report?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Yes, filers may report nonmonetary contributions using a single estimated amount for the entire late period if: (1) in-kind services are received on an ongoing basis; and (2) the value can be estimated with reasonable accuracy. The late report must be filed within 48 hours of receiving the first $1,000 in services, and if actual amounts substantially differ from estimates, an amended report must be filed within 48 hours.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes incoming request letter dated December 7, 1982 from Kirk Pessner/Dobbs & Nielsen, FPPC response letter dated December 17, 1982 from Jeanne Pritchard, and follow-up memo dated January 28, 1983 clarifying implementation. All three documents present and extracted together. Question and conclusion taken from primary advice letter response.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Pursuant to our telephone conversation of December 1, 1982, I am asking that you confirm in writing that it is permissible for filers who anticipate nonmonetary contributions of $1,000 or more during a late filing period to estimate the value of the nonmonetary contributions once for the entire late filing period and file one late contribution notice.

CONCLUSION: Two general quidelines to apply in determining whether it would be permissible to use this method of reporting are: (1) receipt of the in-kind services are on-going during the late reporting period and would result in repititious reporting; (2) the candidate or committee has the ability to estimate the value for the entire late period with a reasonable degree of accuracy. By filing a late contribution report prior to or no later than 48 hours after receipt of the first $1,000 of in-kind services, and reporting the total amount to be received during the late period, the intent of the law will be fully served by providing information to the voters about the source of contributions received just prior to the election. If the value of the in-kind contribution received during the late period substantially differs from the estimated amount reported, another late contribution report must be filed within 48 hours of learning of the discrepancy, amending the original late report to disclose the correct amount.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to Kirk Pessner's question about reporting methodology for nonmonetary in-kind contributions during late filing periods. The Commission confirms that candidates may file a single late contribution report estimating the total value for the entire period if services are ongoing and reasonably estimable, with amendments required if actual amounts substantially differ.
